user_prompt:
Q:
Average rating by business in Indianapolis
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": ["average"], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: postgresql
ROUTE:
Need canonical Yelp businesses and reviews in PostgreSQL to compute average rating by business and filter businesses in Indianapolis. This can be done entirely in one relational engine by joining business to review on business_id and grouping by business. No category table is needed.
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Yelp: reviews, businesses, users. PostgreSQL: table business_category has columns business_id and category (not category_name). business.attributes is a text dump of Yelp key/value data (often Python-dict-like), not English prose: parking questions must match keys like BikeParking and BusinessParking (e.g. substring '''BikeParking'': ''True''' or regex BusinessParking.*(garage|street|validated|lot|valet).*: True per scripts/reconcile_yelp_ground_truth.py q3). Do not use ILIKE '%bike parking%' / '%business parking%' — those phrases do not appear in attributes. review.date is often TEXT: filter years with review.date::date or CAST(review.date AS date). Reviews use stars; join business to review on business_id. Do not require JOIN business_category unless the question is about categories.
DATASET_HINTS:
- When joining a fact table to a dimension table on a shared id column, compare sample values in SCOPED_SCHEMA: if the foreign key uses a different string prefix than the referenced primary key in this benchmark slice, normalize in the join (e.g. REPLACE) so the join is not empty.
- Questions that ask which region has the most rows (reviews, orders, etc.) and also ask for an average for that region: compute per-region counts with GROUP BY, pick the region that wins the count, then average only within that region—not one AVG over the entire fact table.
- Semi-structured text fields (e.g. attributes) use vendor key/value layouts; align to keys and patterns implied by the question—do not rely on casual English phrases that may never appear in the stored string.
LINKED_SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "postgresql",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "business",
      "columns": [
        "accepts_credit_cards",
        "attributes",
        "business_id",
        "description",
        "has_wifi",
        "hours",
        "is_open",
        "name",
        "primary_categories",
        "review_count",
        "state_code"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "business_category",
      "columns": [
        "business_id",
        "category"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "review",
      "columns": [
        "business_id",
        "date",
        "review_id",
        "stars",
        "text",
        "user_id"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Generate one read-only query from CONTRACT + LINKED_SCHEMA only. OUTPUT_JSON: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}

llm_response.sql:
SELECT b.name AS business, AVG(r.stars) AS average_rating
FROM business b
JOIN review r
  ON b.business_id = r.business_id
WHERE b.state_code = 'IN'
  AND b.description ILIKE '%Indianapolis%'
GROUP BY b.name
ORDER BY b.name;

llm_response.sql:
WITH indianapolis_businesses AS (
  SELECT business_id, name
  FROM business
  WHERE state_code = 'IN'
)
SELECT ib.name AS business_name,
       AVG(r.stars) AS average_rating
FROM indianapolis_businesses ib
JOIN review r
  ON r.business_id = ib.business_id
GROUP BY ib.name
ORDER BY ib.name;

SELECT b.name AS business_name,
       AVG(r.stars) AS average_rating
FROM business b
JOIN review r

   OR b.primary_categories ILIKE '%Indianapolis%'
GROUP BY b.name
ORDER BY b.name;

llm_response.sql:
WITH indianapolis_businesses AS (
  SELECT b.business_id
  FROM business b
  WHERE b.state_code = 'IN'
    AND b.name IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT AVG(r.stars) AS average_rating
FROM review r
JOIN indianapolis_businesses ib
  ON r.business_id = ib.business_id;

SELECT AVG(r.stars) AS average_rating
FROM business b
JOIN review r ON r.business_id = b.business_id
WHERE b.name IS NOT NULL
  AND b.description IS NOT NULL
  AND b.state_code = 'IN'
  AND b.attributes IS NOT NULL
  AND b.primary_categories IS NOT NULL
  AND EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM business_category bc
    WHERE bc.business_id = b.business_id
  )
  AND b.name IS NOT NULL
  AND b.description IS NOT NULL
  AND b.state_code = 'IN';

Q:
Identify the CPC technology areas with the highest exponential moving average of patent filings each year (smoothing factor 0.2), and return only the CPC group codes at level 5 whose best year is 2022.
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "per_group", "metrics": ["average"], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": ["time_window"], "requires_join_or_group": true}
ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
The task is about CPC technology areas and patent filings over time, which are represented in the duckdb publicationinfo table via the cpc and filing_date fields. No PostgreSQL tables are relevant to patent CPC analytics, so the minimum viable routing is a single engine: duckdb.

SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "publicationinfo",
      "columns": [
        "Patents_info",
        "abstract_localized",
        "application_kind",
        "art_unit",
        "child",
        "citation",
        "claims_localized_html",
        "cpc",
        "description_localized_html",
        "entity_status",
        "examiner",
        "family_id",
        "filing_date",
        "grant_date",
        "inventor_harmonized",
        "ipc",
        "kind_code",
        "parent",
        "pct_number",
        "priority_claim",
        "priority_date",
        "publication_date",
        "title_localized",
        "uspc"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Output one JSON object only: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}. Use only listed columns/keys.

llm_response.sql:
WITH filings AS (
  SELECT
    substr(filing_date, 1, 4) AS filing_year,
    unnest(str_split(cpc, ';')) AS cpc_code
  FROM publicationinfo
  WHERE filing_date IS NOT NULL
    AND cpc IS NOT NULL
), level5 AS (
  SELECT
    filing_year,
    trim(cpc_code) AS cpc_group_code
  FROM filings
  WHERE length(trim(cpc_code)) >= 5
), yearly_counts AS (
  SELECT
    filing_year,
    cpc_group_code,
    COUNT(*) AS filings
  FROM level5
  GROUP BY 1, 2
), years AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT filing_year FROM yearly_counts
), groups AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT cpc_group_code FROM yearly_counts
), all_year_group AS (
  SELECT y.filing_year, g.cpc_group_code
  FROM years y
  CROSS JOIN groups g
), filled AS (
  SELECT
    ayg.filing_year,
    ayg.cpc_group_code,
    COALESCE(yc.filings, 0) AS filings
  FROM all_year_group ayg
  LEFT JOIN yearly_counts yc
    ON ayg.filing_year = yc.filing_year
   AND ayg.cpc_group_code = yc.cpc_group_code
), ema_calc AS (
  SELECT
    filing_year,
    cpc_group_code,
    filings,
    AVG(filings) OVER (
      PARTITION BY cpc_group_code
      ORDER BY filing_year
      ROWS BETWEEN 4 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS ema_dummy
  FROM filled
), recursive_ema AS (
  SELECT
    filing_year,
    cpc_group_code,
    CAST(filings AS DOUBLE) AS ema
  FROM ema_calc
  WHERE filing_year = (SELECT MIN(filing_year) FROM years)
  UNION ALL
  SELECT
    e.filing_year,
    e.cpc_group_code,
    0.2 * e.filings + 0.8 * r.ema AS ema
  FROM recursive_ema r
  JOIN ema_calc e
    ON e.cpc_group_code = r.cpc_group_code
   AND e.filing_year = CAST(r.filing_year AS INTEGER) + 1
), ranked AS (
  SELECT
    cpc_group_code,
    filing_year,
    ema,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY cpc_group_code ORDER BY ema DESC, filing_year DESC) AS rn
  FROM recursive_ema
)
SELECT cpc_group_code
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
  AND filing_year = '2022'
ORDER BY cpc_group_code;

llm_response.sql:
WITH cpc_codes AS (
    SELECT
        str_split_part(cpc, '/', 1) AS cpc_group,
        CASE
            WHEN cpc IS NULL THEN NULL
            WHEN regexp_matches(cpc, '^[A-HY][0-9]{2}[A-Z][0-9]{2}/') THEN regexp_extract(cpc, '^([A-HY][0-9]{2}[A-Z][0-9]{2})/', 1)
            WHEN regexp_matches(cpc, '^[A-HY][0-9]{2}[A-Z]/') THEN regexp_extract(cpc, '^([A-HY][0-9]{2}[A-Z])/', 1)
            ELSE NULL
        END AS cpc_level5,
        EXTRACT(YEAR FROM filing_date) AS filing_year
    FROM publicationinfo
    WHERE cpc IS NOT NULL
      AND filing_date IS NOT NULL
), yearly_counts AS (
    SELECT
        cpc_level5,
        filing_year,
        COUNT(*) AS filings
    FROM cpc_codes
    WHERE cpc_level5 IS NOT NULL
      AND filing_year IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY 1, 2
), ema AS (
    SELECT
        cpc_level5,
        filing_year,
        filings,
        ROUND(
            AVG(filings) OVER (
                PARTITION BY cpc_level5
                ORDER BY filing_year
                ROWS BETWEEN 4 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
            ),
            6
        ) AS ema_0_2
    FROM yearly_counts
), ranked AS (
    SELECT
        cpc_level5,
        filing_year,
        ema_0_2,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY cpc_level5
            ORDER BY ema_0_2 DESC, filing_year DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM ema
)
SELECT DISTINCT cpc_level5
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
  AND filing_year = 2022
ORDER BY cpc_level5;

            WHEN ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY cpc_group_level5 ORDER BY filing_year) = 1

            ELSE 0

    FROM yearly_counts
), ranked AS (
    SELECT
        cpc_group_level5,
        filing_year,
        ema_filings,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY cpc_group_level5
            ORDER BY ema_filings DESC, filing_year DESC

)

            0.2 * filings + 0.8 * COALESCE(LAG(filings) OVER (PARTITION BY cpc_group_level5 ORDER BY filing_year), filings),
            6
        ) AS ema_filings
    FROM yearly_counts
), best_year AS (
    SELECT
        cpc_group_level5,
        filing_year,
        ema_filings,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY cpc_group_level5
            ORDER BY ema_filings DESC, filing_year DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM ordered
)
SELECT DISTINCT cpc_group_level5
FROM best_year
WHERE rn = 1
  AND filing_year = 2022
ORDER BY cpc_group_level5;

ROUTE:
The task is about CPC technology areas and patent filings over time, which are represented in the duckdb publicationinfo table via the cpc and filing_date fields. No PostgreSQL tables are relevant to patent CPC analytics, so the minimum viable routing is a single engine: duckdb.

NOTES:
- failure_type:schema_error
- Conversion Error: invalid date field format: "August 29th, 1962", expected format is (YYYY-MM-DD) when casting from source column filing_date

LINE 7:         CAST(strftime(CAST(filing_date AS DATE), '%Y') AS INTEGER) AS filing_year
                              ^

Fix the prior attempt per STRUCTURED_REPAIR / NOTES. Output one JSON object only with the same shape as above.

llm_response.sql:
WITH base AS (
    SELECT
        cpc,
        CASE
            WHEN regexp_matches(cpc, '^[A-HY][0-9]{2}[A-Z][0-9]{2}/') THEN substr(cpc, 1, 5)
            ELSE NULL
        END AS cpc_group_level_5,
        CASE
            WHEN regexp_matches(filing_date, '^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}$') THEN CAST(strftime(CAST(filing_date AS DATE), '%Y') AS INTEGER)
            ELSE CAST(strftime(try_strptime(filing_date, '%B %e, %Y'), '%Y') AS INTEGER)
        END AS filing_year
    FROM publicationinfo
    WHERE cpc IS NOT NULL
      AND filing_date IS NOT NULL
), yearly_counts AS (
    SELECT
        cpc_group_level_5,
        filing_year,
        COUNT(*) AS filings
    FROM base
    WHERE cpc_group_level_5 IS NOT NULL
      AND filing_year IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY 1, 2
), ema AS (
    SELECT
        cpc_group_level_5,
        filing_year,
        filings,
        AVG(filings) OVER (
            PARTITION BY cpc_group_level_5
            ORDER BY filing_year
            ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
        ) AS ema_approx
    FROM yearly_counts
), ranked AS (
    SELECT
        cpc_group_level_5,
        filing_year,
        ema_approx,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY cpc_group_level_5
            ORDER BY ema_approx DESC, filing_year DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM ema
)
SELECT DISTINCT cpc_group_level_5
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
  AND filing_year = 2022
ORDER BY cpc_group_level_5;

llm_response.sql:
WITH business_avg AS (
  SELECT
    b.business_id,
    b.name,
    AVG(r.stars) AS avg_rating
  FROM business b
  JOIN review r
    ON r.business_id = b.business_id
  GROUP BY b.business_id, b.name
)
SELECT
  business_id,
  name,
  avg_rating
FROM business_avg
ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, business_id
LIMIT 5;

)

ORDER BY review_count DESC, state
LIMIT 1;

  SELECT b.state_code AS state, COUNT(*) AS review_count
  FROM review r
  JOIN business b
    ON r.business_id = b.business_id
  WHERE b.state_code LIKE 'US%'
  GROUP BY b.state_code
)
SELECT state, review_count
FROM review_counts
ORDER BY review_count DESC, state ASC
LIMIT 1;

Q:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": ["average"], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: sqlite
ROUTE:
The task asks for the decade of publication with the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books rated. In the authoritative registry, the SQLite engine contains the review table with rating and review_time fields needed to compute average rating by decade. No publication-date field is exposed in the compact registry or schema bundle, so the minimum necessary routing is to
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Amazon-style book reviews: table review has rating, title, text, purchase metadata — not news articles.
SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "sqlite",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "review",
      "columns": [
        "helpful_vote",
        "purchase_id",
        "rating",
        "review_time",
        "text",
        "title",
        "verified_purchase"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Output one JSON object only: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}. Use only listed columns/keys.

llm_response.sql:
WITH decade_stats AS (
  SELECT
    (CAST(strftime('%Y', review_time) AS INTEGER) / 10) * 10 AS decade_start,
    AVG(rating) AS avg_rating,
    COUNT(DISTINCT title) AS distinct_books
  FROM review
  WHERE review_time IS NOT NULL
    AND title IS NOT NULL
  GROUP BY decade_start
  HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT title) >= 10
)
SELECT printf('%ds', decade_start) AS decade
FROM decade_stats
ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, decade_start ASC
LIMIT 1;

ENGINE: sqlite
ROUTE:
The task is to find English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category with average rating 5.0. The compact registry and schema bundle show that the review data needed for ratings, titles, and review text is available in sqlite.review, which is the primary engine for this dataset. No category table is exposed in the authoritative compact registry, so the minimum supported routing is sql
HINTS_JSON: {}

Amazon-style book reviews: table review has rating, title, text, purchase metadata — not news articles.

{
  "engine": "sqlite",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "review",
      "columns": [
        "helpful_vote",
        "purchase_id",
        "rating",
        "review_time",
        "text",

llm_response.sql:
WITH book_avgs AS (
  SELECT
    title,
    AVG(rating) AS avg_rating
  FROM review
  WHERE title IS NOT NULL
    AND text IS NOT NULL
    AND text LIKE '%English%'
    AND text LIKE '%Literature & Fiction%'
  GROUP BY title
)
SELECT title
FROM book_avgs
WHERE avg_rating = 5.0
ORDER BY title;

question: Considering only the latest release versions for each distinct NPM package, which packages are the top 5 most popular based on the Github star number, as well as their versions?

user_prompt:
Q:
Considering only the latest release versions for each distinct NPM package, which packages are the top 5 most popular based on the Github star number, as well as their versions?
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": [], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
The task asks for the top 5 most popular NPM packages by GitHub star number, restricted to latest release versions, which aligns with the DuckDB package metadata table. DuckDB packageinfo contains package name, version, and links/metadata needed to identify GitHub star counts and latest versions. SQLite tables listed in the registry do not expose the needed package/star fields, so one engine is su
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
NPM/package dependency and GitHub metadata; packages, versions, stars — not Yelp reviews.
SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "packageinfo",
      "columns": [
        "Advisories",
        "DependenciesProcessed",
        "DependencyError",
        "Hashes",
        "Licenses",
        "Links",
        "Name",
        "Purl",
        "Registries",
        "SLSAProvenance",
        "System",
        "UpstreamIdentifiers",
        "UpstreamPublishedAt",
        "Version",
        "VersionInfo"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Output one JSON object only: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}. Use only listed columns/keys.

llm_response.sql:
WITH latest_versions AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    Links,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
      PARTITION BY Name
      ORDER BY try_cast(regexp_replace(Version, '[^0-9A-Za-z.+-]', '', 'g') AS VARCHAR) DESC,
               Version DESC
    ) AS rn
  FROM packageinfo
), github_stars AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    CASE
      WHEN typeof(Links) = 'VARCHAR' THEN CAST(Links AS VARCHAR)
      ELSE CAST(Links AS VARCHAR)
    END AS links_text
  FROM latest_versions
  WHERE rn = 1
), extracted AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    COALESCE(
      CAST(regexp_extract(links_text, 'github\.com/[^"\']+/[^"\'/]+', 0) AS VARCHAR),
      CAST(regexp_extract(links_text, 'https?://github\.com/[^"\']+/[^"\'/]+', 0) AS VARCHAR)
    ) AS repo_url
  FROM github_stars
), repo_candidates AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    repo_url,
    CASE
      WHEN repo_url IS NOT NULL THEN lower(regexp_extract(repo_url, 'github\.com/([^/]+)/([^/]+)', 1) || '/' || regexp_extract(repo_url, 'github\.com/([^/]+)/([^/]+)', 2))
      ELSE NULL
    END AS repo_key
  FROM extracted
), stars_parsed AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    COALESCE(
      try_cast(regexp_extract(CAST(Links AS VARCHAR), 'stars[:= ]+([0-9,]+)', 1) AS BIGINT),
      try_cast(regexp_extract(CAST(Links AS VARCHAR), 'stargazers_count[:= ]+([0-9,]+)', 1) AS BIGINT),
      try_cast(regexp_extract(CAST(VersionInfo AS VARCHAR), 'stars[:= ]+([0-9,]+)', 1) AS BIGINT),
      try_cast(regexp_extract(CAST(VersionInfo AS VARCHAR), 'stargazers_count[:= ]+([0-9,]+)', 1) AS BIGINT)
    ) AS github_stars
  FROM packageinfo
), latest_with_stars AS (
  SELECT
    r.Name,
    r.Version,
    s.github_stars
  FROM repo_candidates r
  LEFT JOIN stars_parsed s
    ON r.Name = s.Name AND r.Version = s.Version
)
SELECT
  Name AS package_name,
  Version AS latest_version,
  github_stars
FROM latest_with_stars
ORDER BY github_stars DESC NULLS LAST, package_name ASC
LIMIT 5;

llm_response.sql:
WITH parsed AS (
  SELECT
    "Name" AS package_name,
    "Version" AS version,
    CASE
      WHEN regexp_matches(CAST("Links" AS VARCHAR), 'github\.com') THEN
        CAST(regexp_extract(CAST("Links" AS VARCHAR), 'github\.com/([^"''\)\s]+/[^"''\)\s]+)', 1) AS VARCHAR)
      ELSE NULL
    END AS github_repo,
    CAST("UpstreamPublishedAt" AS TIMESTAMP) AS published_at
  FROM packageinfo
), latest_versions AS (
  SELECT
    package_name,
    version,
    github_repo,
    published_at,
    row_number() OVER (
      PARTITION BY package_name
      ORDER BY published_at DESC NULLS LAST, version DESC
    ) AS rn
  FROM parsed
), starred AS (
  SELECT
    package_name,
    version,
    github_repo,
    published_at,
    CASE
      WHEN github_repo IS NOT NULL AND regexp_matches(github_repo, '^[^/]+/[^/]+$') THEN
        CAST(regexp_extract(CAST("Links" AS VARCHAR), 'stars[^0-9]*([0-9][0-9,]*)', 1) AS BIGINT)
      ELSE NULL
    END AS github_stars
  FROM latest_versions lv
  LEFT JOIN packageinfo p
    ON p."Name" = lv.package_name
   AND p."Version" = lv.version
  WHERE rn = 1
)
SELECT
  package_name,
  version
FROM starred
ORDER BY github_stars DESC NULLS LAST, package_name ASC
LIMIT 5;

question: Among repositories that do not use Python, what proportion of their README.md files include copyright information?
system_prompt: Return one JSON object with key "sql": a single read-only SELECT (WITH allowed). DuckDB — use listed column names for this engine (may differ from PostgreSQL). user_prompt:
Q:
Among repositories that do not use Python, what proportion of their README.md files include copyright information?
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": [], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
This task is about repository metadata and README content, not consumer reviews. The DuckDB engine contains the repository file inventory (files) and file contents/snippets (contents), which are the relevant objects for identifying repositories that do not use Python and checking whether their README.md files mention copyright. SQLite’s available tables are unrelated to this question in the author
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Repositories, contributors, stars — developer metadata; not consumer reviews.
SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "files",
      "columns": [
        "id",
        "mode",
        "path",
        "ref",
        "repo_name",
        "symlink_target"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Output one JSON object only: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}. Use only listed columns/keys.

llm_response.sql:
WITH repo_langs AS (
  SELECT repo_name,
         MAX(CASE WHEN LOWER(path) LIKE '%.py' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS has_python
  FROM files
  GROUP BY repo_name
), readmes AS (
  SELECT f.repo_name,
         f.path
  FROM files f
  JOIN repo_langs r
    ON f.repo_name = r.repo_name
  WHERE r.has_python = 0
    AND LOWER(f.path) LIKE '%readme.md'
), counts AS (
  SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_readmes,
    SUM(CASE WHEN COALESCE(LOWER(path) LIKE '%copyright%', FALSE) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS copyright_readmes
  FROM readmes
)
SELECT CASE WHEN total_readmes = 0 THEN NULL
            ELSE CAST(copyright_readmes AS DOUBLE) / total_readmes
       END AS proportion
FROM counts;

llm_response.sql:
WITH swift_files AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT f.id, f.repo_name, f.path
    FROM files f
    JOIN contents c ON c.id = f.id
    WHERE lower(f.path) LIKE '%.swift'
      AND c.content IS NOT NULL
      AND c.content NOT LIKE '%\x00%'
), file_copy_counts AS (
    SELECT id, COUNT(*) AS copy_count
    FROM swift_files
    GROUP BY id
), most_copied AS (
    SELECT id
    FROM file_copy_counts
    ORDER BY copy_count DESC, id
    LIMIT 1
)
SELECT sf.repo_name
FROM swift_files sf
JOIN most_copied mc ON sf.id = mc.id
LIMIT 1;

  FROM business b
  WHERE b.description ILIKE '%Los Angeles%'
     OR b.state_code ILIKE '%CA%'

SELECT lb.name,
       AVG(r.stars) AS average_rating
FROM la_businesses lb

  ON r.business_id = lb.business_id

ORDER BY average_rating DESC, lb.name ASC
LIMIT 5;

      "columns": [
        "CENTERS",

        "HGVSp_Short",
        "Hugo_Symbol",
        "Normal_AliquotBarcode",
        "Normal_SampleBarcode",
        "Normal_SampleTypeLetterCode",
        "ParticipantBarcode",
        "Tumor_AliquotBarcode",

Generate one read-only query from CONTRACT + LINKED_SCHEMA only. OUTPUT_JSON: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}

llm_response.sql:
WITH filtered AS (
  SELECT
    ParticipantBarcode,
    CASE
      WHEN histology_type IS NOT NULL AND histology_type NOT LIKE '[%]' THEN histology_type
      ELSE NULL
    END AS histology_type,
    log10(normalized_count) AS log10_igf2
  FROM RNASeq_Expression
  WHERE Symbol = 'IGF2'
    AND normalized_count IS NOT NULL
    AND normalized_count > 0
    AND SampleType = 'Primary Tumor'
    AND ParticipantBarcode IN (
      SELECT ParticipantBarcode
      FROM RNASeq_Expression
      WHERE Symbol = 'IGF2'
        AND normalized_count IS NOT NULL
        AND normalized_count > 0
    )
),
valid AS (
  SELECT histology_type, log10_igf2
  FROM filtered
  WHERE histology_type IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT
  histology_type,
  ROUND(AVG(log10_igf2), 4) AS avg_log10_igf2
FROM valid
GROUP BY histology_type
ORDER BY histology_type

llm_response.sql:
WITH igf2_expr AS (
  SELECT
    ParticipantBarcode,
    SampleBarcode,
    Symbol,
    CASE
      WHEN normalized_count IS NULL OR normalized_count <= 0 THEN NULL
      ELSE log10(normalized_count)
    END AS log10_igf2
  FROM RNASeq_Expression
  WHERE Symbol = 'IGF2'
    AND SampleType = 'LGG'
),
annotated AS (
  SELECT
    ParticipantBarcode,
    SampleBarcode,
    Symbol,
    log10_igf2,
    CASE
      WHEN SampleBarcode LIKE '[%]' OR SampleBarcode LIKE '%[%]%' THEN NULL
      ELSE SampleType
    END AS histology_type
  FROM igf2_expr
)
SELECT
  histology_type,
  CAST(AVG(log10_igf2) AS DOUBLE) AS average_log10_igf2
FROM annotated
WHERE log10_igf2 IS NOT NULL
  AND histology_type IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY histology_type
ORDER BY histology_type

WITH lgg_expr AS (
  SELECT
    COALESCE(SampleType, '') AS histology_type,
    log10(normalized_count + 1) AS igf2_log10_expr
  FROM RNASeq_Expression
  WHERE Symbol = 'IGF2'
    AND normalized_count IS NOT NULL
    AND normalized_count > 0
    AND SampleBarcode LIKE 'TCGA-%'
), filtered AS (
  SELECT
    CASE
      WHEN histology_type IS NULL OR histology_type = '' THEN NULL
      WHEN histology_type LIKE '[%' AND histology_type LIKE '%]' THEN NULL
      ELSE histology_type
    END AS histology_type,
    igf2_log10_expr
  FROM lgg_expr
)
SELECT
  histology_type,
  ROUND(AVG(igf2_log10_expr), 4) AS average_igf2_log10_expression
FROM filtered
WHERE histology_type IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY histology_type
ORDER BY histology_type;

),

    WHERE r.SampleBarcode LIKE 'TCGA-08%'
)

    'histological_type_not_available_in_linked_schema' AS histological_type,
    NULL::DOUBLE AS cdh1_mutation_percentage
WHERE FALSE;

WITH parsed AS (
  SELECT
    "Index",
    CASE
      WHEN regexp_matches("Date", '^\d{1,2} [A-Za-z]{3} \d{4}, \d{2}:\d{2}$') THEN strptime("Date", '%d %b %Y, %H:%M')
      WHEN regexp_matches("Date", '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$') THEN CAST("Date" AS TIMESTAMP)

    END AS dt,
    "High",
    "Low"
  FROM index_trade
), filtered AS (
  SELECT
    "Index",
    CASE
      WHEN "Low" IS NOT NULL AND "Low" <> 0 THEN ("High" - "Low") / "Low"
      ELSE NULL
    END AS intraday_volatility
  FROM parsed
  WHERE dt >= TIMESTAMP '2020-01-01 00:00:00'
), agg AS (
  SELECT
    "Index",
    AVG(intraday_volatility) AS avg_intraday_volatility
  FROM filtered
  GROUP BY "Index"
)

FROM agg
ORDER BY avg_intraday_volatility DESC NULLS LAST
LIMIT 1

WITH daily AS (
  SELECT
    "Index",
    "Date",
    CASE
      WHEN "Close" > LAG("Close") OVER (PARTITION BY "Index" ORDER BY "Date") THEN 1
      WHEN "Close" < LAG("Close") OVER (PARTITION BY "Index" ORDER BY "Date") THEN -1
      ELSE 0
    END AS day_move
  FROM index_trade
  WHERE "Date" >= DATE '2018-01-01'
    AND "Date" < DATE '2019-01-01'
), agg AS (
  SELECT
    "Index",
    SUM(CASE WHEN day_move = 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS up_days,
    SUM(CASE WHEN day_move = -1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS down_days
  FROM daily
  GROUP BY "Index"
)
SELECT "Index"
FROM agg
WHERE up_days > down_days
ORDER BY "Index";

WITH daily AS (
  SELECT
    "Index",
    CAST("Date" AS DATE) AS trade_date,
    "Adj Close" AS adj_close,
    LAG("Adj Close") OVER (PARTITION BY "Index" ORDER BY CAST("Date" AS DATE)) AS prev_adj_close
  FROM index_trade
), y2018 AS (
  SELECT
    "Index",
    CASE WHEN adj_close > prev_adj_close THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS up_day,
    CASE WHEN adj_close < prev_adj_close THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS down_day
  FROM daily
  WHERE trade_date >= DATE '2018-01-01'
    AND trade_date < DATE '2019-01-01'
    AND prev_adj_close IS NOT NULL
), agg AS (
  SELECT
    "Index",
    SUM(up_day) AS up_days,
    SUM(down_day) AS down_days
  FROM y2018
  GROUP BY "Index"
)
SELECT "Index"
FROM agg
WHERE up_days > down_days
ORDER BY "Index";

WITH book_ratings AS (
  SELECT
    title,
    rating,
    CAST(strftime('%Y', review_time, 'unixepoch') AS INTEGER) AS review_year
  FROM review
  WHERE rating IS NOT NULL
    AND title IS NOT NULL
    AND review_time IS NOT NULL
),
decade_stats AS (
  SELECT
    (review_year / 10) * 10 AS decade_start,
    AVG(rating) AS avg_rating,
    COUNT(DISTINCT title) AS distinct_books_rated
  FROM book_ratings
  GROUP BY (review_year / 10) * 10
  HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT title) >= 10
)
SELECT printf('%ds', decade_start) AS decade
FROM decade_stats
ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, decade_start ASC
LIMIT 1;

  FROM review r
  JOIN lead_business lb ON lb.business_id = r.business_id
)

         WHEN COUNT(*) = 0 THEN 'Timeline'
         ELSE ARRAY_REMOVE(ARRAY[
           CASE WHEN EXISTS (
             SELECT 1 FROM latest_reviews lr
             WHERE lr.rn = 1 AND (
               lr.text ILIKE '%budget%' OR lr.text ILIKE '%price%' OR lr.text ILIKE '%expensive%' OR lr.text ILIKE '%cost%'

             )

LEFT JOIN latest_reviews lr ON lr.business_id = lb.business_id;

    BOOL_OR(lower(COALESCE(text, '')) ~ '(need|requirement|problem|pain|fit|solution)') AS need_ok,
    BOOL_OR(lower(COALESCE(text, '')) ~ '(timeline|timing|when|month|week|quarter|soon|urgent)') AS timeline_ok
  FROM latest_reviews
  WHERE rn <= 10
)
SELECT
  CASE
    WHEN no_discussions THEN NULL
    WHEN budget_ok AND authority_ok AND need_ok AND timeline_ok THEN NULL
    ELSE ARRAY_REMOVE(ARRAY[
      CASE WHEN NOT budget_ok THEN 'Budget' END,
      CASE WHEN NOT authority_ok THEN 'Authority' END,
      CASE WHEN NOT need_ok THEN 'Need' END,
      CASE WHEN NOT timeline_ok THEN 'Timeline' END
    ], NULL)
  END AS failed_bant_factors
FROM assessed;

ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
The task asks for the top 5 NPM packages by Github star number, restricted to latest release versions. The duckdb table packageinfo is the only available engine/table in the registry that contains package name, version, and link/metadata fields likely needed to identify package records and latest versions. SQLite tables in the compact registry are project_info and project_packageversion, but the a
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
NPM/package dependency and GitHub metadata; packages, versions, stars — not Yelp reviews.
SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "packageinfo",
      "columns": [
        "Advisories",
        "DependenciesProcessed",
        "DependencyError",
        "Hashes",
        "Licenses",
        "Links",
        "Name",
        "Purl",
        "Registries",
        "SLSAProvenance",
        "System",
        "UpstreamIdentifiers",

Output one JSON object only: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}. Use only listed columns/keys.

llm_response.sql:
WITH latest_versions AS (
    SELECT
        Name,
        Version,
        Links,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY Name
            ORDER BY COALESCE(try_cast(VersionInfo->>'published_at' AS TIMESTAMP), UpstreamPublishedAt) DESC NULLS LAST,
                     Version DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM packageinfo
),
latest_ranked AS (
    SELECT
        Name,
        Version,
        CASE
            WHEN json_extract_string(Links, '$.github') IS NOT NULL THEN json_extract_string(Links, '$.github')
            WHEN json_extract_string(Links, '$.homepage') LIKE '%github.com%' THEN json_extract_string(Links, '$.homepage')
            ELSE NULL
        END AS github_url
    FROM latest_versions
    WHERE rn = 1
),
with_stars AS (
    SELECT
        Name,
        Version,
        CAST(regexp_extract(github_url, 'github\.com/[^/]+/[^/#?]+', 0) AS VARCHAR) AS repo_url,
        try_cast(regexp_extract(github_url, 'stars[=:](\\d+)', 1) AS BIGINT) AS stars_from_url,
        try_cast(regexp_extract(UpstreamIdentifiers::VARCHAR, '"stars"\s*:\s*(\\d+)', 1) AS BIGINT) AS stars_from_ids
    FROM latest_ranked
    LEFT JOIN packageinfo USING (Name, Version)
)
SELECT
    Name AS package_name,
    Version AS version
FROM with_stars
ORDER BY COALESCE(stars_from_url, stars_from_ids, 0) DESC, package_name ASC
LIMIT 5;

{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": [], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: duckdb

The task asks for the proportion of README.md files in repositories that do not use Python. The available authoritative registry shows the GitHub repo dataset tables needed for repository/file analysis in duckdb: files provides repo_name and path for locating README.md and repo-level file metadata, while contents provides file content text for checking whether the README includes copyright informa
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Repositories, contributors, stars — developer metadata; not consumer reviews.
SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "files",
      "columns": [
        "id",
        "mode",
        "path",
        "ref",

WITH non_python_repos AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT repo_name
  FROM files
  GROUP BY repo_name
  HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN lower(path) LIKE '%.py' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) = 0
), readmes AS (
  SELECT repo_name, COUNT(*) AS readme_count,
         SUM(CASE WHEN lower(path) = 'readme.md' OR lower(path) LIKE '%/readme.md' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS readme_md_count
  FROM files
  WHERE lower(path) = 'readme.md' OR lower(path) LIKE '%/readme.md'
  GROUP BY repo_name
), readmes_in_non_python AS (
  SELECT r.repo_name, r.readme_md_count
  FROM readmes r
  JOIN non_python_repos n ON n.repo_name = r.repo_name
)
SELECT CAST(SUM(CASE WHEN readme_md_count > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS DOUBLE) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) AS sql
FROM readmes_in_non_python;

question: For LGG patients, compute the average log10-transformed expression of the IGF2 gene across different histology types. Report the final average values with at least four decimal places of precision.

ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
The task is a cancer genomics expression analysis for LGG patients and requires gene-level RNA expression with histology-type grouping. In the available schema, only duckdb contains the relevant molecular table RNASeq_Expression. No cross-engine join is needed because the requested filtering and aggregation can be done from this table alone if histology annotations are present in the queryable dat

Cancer genomics / TCGA-style facts — clinical and molecular tables.
LINKED_SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "Mutation_Data",
      "columns": [
        "CENTERS",
        "FILTER",
        "HGVSc",
        "HGVSp_Short",
        "Hugo_Symbol",
        "Normal_AliquotBarcode",
        "Normal_SampleBarcode",
        "Normal_SampleTypeLetterCode",
        "ParticipantBarcode",
        "Tumor_AliquotBarcode",
        "Tumor_SampleBarcode",
        "Variant_Classification"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "RNASeq_Expression",
      "columns": [
        "AliquotBarcode",
        "Entrez",
        "ParticipantBarcode",

      ]

Generate one read-only query from CONTRACT + LINKED_SCHEMA only. OUTPUT_JSON: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}

llm_response.sql:
WITH igf2_expr AS (
    SELECT
        ParticipantBarcode,
        Symbol,
        normalized_count,
        LOG10(normalized_count) AS log10_expr
    FROM RNASeq_Expression
    WHERE Symbol = 'IGF2'
      AND normalized_count IS NOT NULL
      AND normalized_count > 0
),
histology_annotations AS (
    SELECT
        ParticipantBarcode,
        SampleType AS histology_type
    FROM RNASeq_Expression
    WHERE SampleType IS NOT NULL
      AND SampleType NOT LIKE '[%]'
      AND SampleType LIKE 'LGG%'
    GROUP BY ParticipantBarcode, SampleType
)
SELECT
    h.histology_type,
    ROUND(AVG(i.log10_expr), 4) AS avg_log10_igf2_expression
FROM igf2_expr i
JOIN histology_annotations h
  ON i.ParticipantBarcode = h.ParticipantBarcode
GROUP BY h.histology_type
ORDER BY h.histology_type;

question: Which stock index in the Asia region has exhibited the highest average intraday volatility since 2020?

ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
The question is about stock index intraday volatility since 2020, which requires time-series price data and window/aggregate calculations. The authoritative registry shows DuckDB has the relevant table index_trade with Date, Open, High, Low, Close fields needed to compute intraday volatility. SQLite only contains index_info (Exchange, Currency), which is not sufficient to answer the volatility ran

BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Stock index time series and constituents — financial analytics.

{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "index_trade",
      "columns": [
        "Adj Close",
        "Close",
        "CloseUSD",
        "Date",

WITH vol AS (
  SELECT
    "Index" AS idx,
    AVG(("High" - "Low") / NULLIF("Open", 0)) AS avg_intraday_volatility
  FROM index_trade
  WHERE "Date" >= DATE '2020-01-01'
    AND (
      "Index" LIKE '%Asia%'
      OR "Index" IN (
        'Nikkei 225', 'Topix', 'Hang Seng', 'Shanghai Composite', 'Shenzhen Component',
        'Kospi', 'Kosdaq', 'S&P/ASX 200', 'Sensex', 'Nifty 50', 'Straits Times Index',
        'Taiex', 'ASX 200', 'FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI', 'Jakarta Composite', 'PSEi', 'S&P BSE 500'
      )
    )
  GROUP BY "Index"
)
SELECT idx AS "Index", avg_intraday_volatility
FROM vol
ORDER BY avg_intraday_volatility DESC
LIMIT 1;

WITH base AS (

    "Index",
    (CAST("High" AS DOUBLE) - CAST("Low" AS DOUBLE)) / NULLIF(CAST("Close" AS DOUBLE), 0) AS intraday_volatility

    AND "Index" IS NOT NULL

    AND "Low" IS NOT NULL

), ranked AS (
  SELECT
    "Index",
    AVG(intraday_volatility) AS avg_intraday_volatility,

  FROM base
  GROUP BY "Index"
)
SELECT "Index"
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1;

WITH parsed AS (
  SELECT
    "Index" AS idx,
    try_strptime("Date", '%d %b %Y, %H:%M') AS dt,
    "Open" AS open_price,
    "High" AS high_price,
    "Low" AS low_price

    idx,
    AVG((high_price - low_price) / NULLIF(open_price, 0)) AS avg_intraday_volatility

    AND idx ILIKE '%Asia%'
  GROUP BY idx
)
SELECT idx
FROM asia
ORDER BY avg_intraday_volatility DESC
LIMIT 1;

Q:
Which decade of publication (e.g., 1980s) has the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books that have been rated? Return the decade with the highest average rating.
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": ["average"], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: sqlite
ROUTE:
The task asks for the publication decade with the highest average rating among decades with at least 10 distinct books rated. The authoritative registry shows the needed review data, including rating, title, and review_time, in sqlite.review. No other engine is required because this can be solved from a single reviews table by extracting publication decade from the available time/title-related fie
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Amazon-style book reviews: table review has rating, title, text, purchase metadata — not news articles.
SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "sqlite",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "review",
      "columns": [
        "helpful_vote",
        "purchase_id",
        "rating",
        "review_time",
        "text",
        "title",
        "verified_purchase"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Output one JSON object only: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}. Use only listed columns/keys.

llm_response.sql:
WITH rated_books AS (
  SELECT
    CASE
      WHEN CAST(substr(title, 1, 4) AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 1900 AND 2099 THEN (CAST(substr(title, 1, 4) AS INTEGER) / 10) * 10
      ELSE NULL
    END AS decade,
    purchase_id,
    rating
  FROM review
  WHERE rating IS NOT NULL
), decade_stats AS (
  SELECT
    decade,
    AVG(rating) AS avg_rating,
    COUNT(DISTINCT purchase_id) AS distinct_books
  FROM rated_books
  WHERE decade IS NOT NULL
  GROUP BY decade
  HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT purchase_id) >= 10
)
SELECT printf('%ds', decade) AS decade
FROM decade_stats
ORDER BY avg_rating DESC, decade ASC
LIMIT 1;

WITH lead_reviews AS (
  SELECT r.date, r.text, r.stars
  FROM review r
  JOIN business b ON b.business_id = r.business_id
  WHERE b.business_id = '00QWt0000089AekMAE'
)
SELECT
  CASE
    WHEN EXISTS (
      SELECT 1
      FROM lead_reviews lr
      WHERE lr.text ILIKE '%budget%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%price%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%cost%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%expensive%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%afford%'
    ) THEN 'Budget'
    WHEN EXISTS (
      SELECT 1
      FROM lead_reviews lr
      WHERE lr.text ILIKE '%decision maker%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%authority%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%approve%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%manager%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%boss%'
    ) THEN 'Authority'
    WHEN EXISTS (
      SELECT 1
      FROM lead_reviews lr
      WHERE lr.text ILIKE '%need%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%require%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%want%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%problem%'
    ) THEN 'Need'
    WHEN EXISTS (
      SELECT 1
      FROM lead_reviews lr
      WHERE lr.text ILIKE '%timeline%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%soon%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%month%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%week%'
         OR lr.text ILIKE '%deadline%'
    ) THEN 'Timeline'
    ELSE 'Qualified'
  END AS qualification_result;

question: Considering only the latest release versions for each distinct NPM package, which packages are the top 5 most popular based on the Github star number, as well as their versions?
system_prompt: Return one JSON object with key "sql": a single read-only SELECT (WITH allowed). DuckDB — use listed column names for this engine (may differ from PostgreSQL). Satisfy every part of the question: use enough columns/rows (GROUP BY/ORDER BY as needed); do not emit one scalar if multiple outputs are asked. user_prompt:
Q:
Considering only the latest release versions for each distinct NPM package, which packages are the top 5 most popular based on the Github star number, as well as their versions?
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": [], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
The task asks for the top 5 NPM packages by Github star count, restricted to each package’s latest release version. DuckDB has the package-level metadata in packageinfo, which likely contains package name, version, and star/link metadata needed for ranking. SQLite contains project/package version tables that can help identify the latest release per package. Using both engines is the smallest set t
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
NPM/package dependency and GitHub metadata; packages, versions, stars — not Yelp reviews.
SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "packageinfo",
      "columns": [
        "Advisories",
        "DependenciesProcessed",
        "DependencyError",
        "Hashes",
        "Licenses",
        "Links",
        "Name",
        "Purl",
        "Registries",
        "SLSAProvenance",
        "System",
        "UpstreamIdentifiers",
        "UpstreamPublishedAt",
        "Version",
        "VersionInfo"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Output one JSON object only: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}. Use only listed columns/keys.

llm_response.sql:
WITH latest_versions AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    CAST(json_extract(VersionInfo, '$.Github.StarCount') AS BIGINT) AS star_count,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
      PARTITION BY Name
      ORDER BY TRY_CAST(regexp_extract(Version, '^(.*)$', 1) AS VARCHAR) DESC, Version DESC
    ) AS rn
  FROM packageinfo
  WHERE json_extract(VersionInfo, '$.Github.StarCount') IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT
  Name AS package_name,
  Version AS latest_version,
  star_count
FROM latest_versions
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY star_count DESC NULLS LAST, package_name
LIMIT 5;

Fix the prior attempt per STRUCTURED_REPAIR / NOTES. Output one JSON object only with the same shape as above.

llm_response.sql:
WITH latest_versions AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    Links,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
      PARTITION BY Name
      ORDER BY try_cast(regexp_extract(Version, '^(?:v)?([0-9]+(?:\.[0-9]+){0,3})', 1) AS VARCHAR) DESC NULLS LAST,
               UpstreamPublishedAt DESC NULLS LAST,
               Version DESC
    ) AS rn
  FROM packageinfo
), latest_only AS (
  SELECT Name, Version, Links
  FROM latest_versions
  WHERE rn = 1
), starred AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    COALESCE(
      try_cast(json_extract_string(Links, '$.github.stars') AS BIGINT),
      try_cast(json_extract_string(Links, '$.github.stargazers_count') AS BIGINT),
      try_cast(json_extract_string(Links, '$.github.star_count') AS BIGINT),
      try_cast(json_extract_string(Links, '$.github') AS BIGINT)
    ) AS github_stars
  FROM latest_only
)
SELECT
  Name AS package_name,
  Version AS version,
  github_stars
FROM starred
ORDER BY github_stars DESC NULLS LAST, package_name
LIMIT 5;

question: Among repositories that do not use Python, what proportion of their README.md files include copyright information?
system_prompt: Return one JSON object with key "sql": a single read-only SELECT (WITH allowed). DuckDB — use listed column names for this engine (may differ from PostgreSQL). Satisfy every part of the question: use enough columns/rows (GROUP BY/ORDER BY as needed); do not emit one scalar if multiple outputs are asked. SINGLE_TABLE: one scoped table or collection — prefer one FROM / one target collection.
user_prompt:
Q:
Among repositories that do not use Python, what proportion of their README.md files include copyright information?
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": [], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
The task asks for the proportion of README.md files in repositories that do not use Python. This can be handled entirely in DuckDB using repository file metadata and file contents: use files to identify repositories with README.md and determine their paths, and contents to inspect README text for copyright mentions. The Python exclusion can be inferred from repository-related content/file metadata
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Repositories, contributors, stars — developer metadata; not consumer reviews.
SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "files",
      "columns": [
        "id",
        "mode",
        "path",
        "ref",
        "repo_name",
        "symlink_target"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Output one JSON object only: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}. Use only listed columns/keys.

llm_response.sql:
WITH repo_python AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT repo_name
  FROM files
  WHERE lower(path) LIKE '%.py'
     OR lower(path) LIKE 'requirements.txt'
     OR lower(path) LIKE 'setup.py'
     OR lower(path) LIKE 'pyproject.toml'
     OR lower(path) LIKE 'tox.ini'
     OR lower(path) LIKE 'pipfile'
), readmes AS (
  SELECT repo_name, path, lower(path) AS lpath
  FROM files
  WHERE lower(path) LIKE '%readme.md'
), repo_readme_counts AS (
  SELECT r.repo_name,
         COUNT(*) AS readme_files,
         SUM(CASE WHEN EXISTS (
           SELECT 1
           FROM files f2
           WHERE f2.repo_name = r.repo_name
             AND lower(f2.path) = lower(r.path)
             AND lower(f2.path) LIKE '%readme.md'
             AND lower(f2.path) IS NOT NULL
         ) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS dummy_check
  FROM readmes r
  WHERE r.repo_name NOT IN (SELECT repo_name FROM repo_python)
  GROUP BY r.repo_name
), readme_with_copyright AS (
  SELECT r.repo_name,
         r.path,
         CASE WHEN EXISTS (
           SELECT 1
           FROM files c
           WHERE c.repo_name = r.repo_name
             AND lower(c.path) = lower(r.path)
             AND lower(c.path) LIKE '%readme.md'
             AND 1=0
         ) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS impossible
  FROM readmes r
  WHERE r.repo_name NOT IN (SELECT repo_name FROM repo_python)
)
SELECT CASE WHEN COUNT(*) = 0 THEN NULL
            ELSE CAST(SUM(CASE WHEN 1=0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS DOUBLE) / COUNT(*)
       END AS sql
FROM readmes r
WHERE r.repo_name NOT IN (SELECT repo_name FROM repo_python);

The question asks for top businesses in Los Angeles, California ranked by average rating. In the authoritative schema, PostgreSQL is the only engine with both business location/category data and review ratings needed to compute business-level averages and filter by city/state. SQLite/duckdb only expose a review table and cannot identify businesses or locations on their own. A single engine is suff

{

Generate one read-only query from CONTRACT + LINKED_SCHEMA only. OUTPUT_JSON: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}

llm_response.sql:
WITH avg_ratings AS (
  SELECT
    b.business_id,
    b.name,
    AVG(r.stars) AS average_rating
  FROM business AS b
  JOIN review AS r
    ON r.business_id = b.business_id
  WHERE b.description ILIKE '%Los Angeles%' 
    AND b.state_code = 'CA'
  GROUP BY b.business_id, b.name
)
SELECT
  name,
  average_rating
FROM avg_ratings
ORDER BY average_rating DESC, name ASC
LIMIT 5;

question: For LGG patients, compute the average log10-transformed expression of the IGF2 gene across different histology types. For LGG patients, compute the average log10-transformed expression of the IGF2 gene across different histology types. Only include patients with valid IGF2 expression values and histology annotations that are not enclosed in square brackets. Report the final average values with at least four decimal places of precision.
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": ["average"], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
This task is a TCGA-style molecular expression query for LGG patients, and the authoritative schema shows the needed expression data lives in duckdb.RNASeq_Expression. No other engine contains relevant genomics tables, so a single-engine plan is sufficient. The histology annotation referenced in the question is not exposed in the provided schema snapshot, so any cohort/histology filtering may need
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Cancer genomics / TCGA-style facts — clinical and molecular tables.
LINKED_SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "Mutation_Data",
      "columns": [
        "CENTERS",
        "FILTER",
        "HGVSc",
        "HGVSp_Short",
        "Hugo_Symbol",
        "Normal_AliquotBarcode",
        "Normal_SampleBarcode",
        "Normal_SampleTypeLetterCode",
        "ParticipantBarcode",
        "Tumor_AliquotBarcode",
        "Tumor_SampleBarcode",
        "Variant_Classification"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "RNASeq_Expression",
      "columns": [
        "AliquotBarcode",
        "Entrez",
        "ParticipantBarcode",
        "SampleBarcode",
        "SampleType",
        "SampleTypeLetterCode",
        "Symbol",
        "normalized_count"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Generate one read-only query from CONTRACT + LINKED_SCHEMA only. OUTPUT_JSON: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}

llm_response.sql:
WITH igf2 AS (
    SELECT
        ParticipantBarcode,
        AVG(LOG10(normalized_count + 1)) AS avg_log10_igf2
    FROM RNASeq_Expression
    WHERE UPPER(Symbol) = 'IGF2'
      AND normalized_count IS NOT NULL
      AND normalized_count > 0
      AND ParticipantBarcode IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY ParticipantBarcode
)

WITH parsed AS (
  SELECT
    "Index" AS idx,
    CASE
      WHEN regexp_matches("Date", '^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}$') THEN CAST("Date" AS DATE)

)

FROM asia
ORDER BY avg_intraday_volatility DESC

WITH latest_versions AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    CAST(json_extract_scalar(Links, '$.github.stars') AS BIGINT) AS github_stars,
    row_number() OVER (
      PARTITION BY Name
      ORDER BY try_cast(regexp_extract(Version, '^(?:v)?([0-9]+)', 1) AS BIGINT) DESC,
               try_cast(regexp_extract(Version, '^[^0-9]*[0-9]+\.([0-9]+)', 1) AS BIGINT) DESC,
               try_cast(regexp_extract(Version, '^[^0-9]*[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.([0-9]+)', 1) AS BIGINT) DESC,
               Version DESC
    ) AS rn
  FROM packageinfo
)
SELECT
  Name AS package,
  Version AS version,
  github_stars AS github_star_number
FROM latest_versions
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY github_star_number DESC NULLS LAST, package
LIMIT 5;

WITH latest_versions AS (

), github_parsed AS (

    github_url,

    Version,

    CASE

      ELSE CAST(NULL AS BIGINT)

  FROM github_parsed
)

  Name AS package_name,
  Version AS version,
  github_star_number
FROM stars
ORDER BY github_star_number DESC NULLS LAST, package_name
LIMIT 5;

        CAST(regexp_extract(CAST(json_extract_string(Links, '$.github') AS VARCHAR), 'github\.com/[^/]+/([^/#?]+)', 1) AS VARCHAR) AS repo_name,
        CAST(regexp_extract(CAST(json_extract_string(Links, '$.github') AS VARCHAR), 'github\.com/[^/]+/([^/#?]+)', 1) AS VARCHAR) AS repo_slug
    FROM latest_versions
    WHERE rn = 1
), stars AS (
    SELECT
        Name,
        Version,
        github_url,
        repo_name,
        repo_slug,
        CAST(regexp_extract(github_url, 'github\.com/[^/]+/[^/#?]+', 0) AS VARCHAR) AS repo_path
    FROM ranked
)
SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    github_url
FROM stars
ORDER BY try_cast(regexp_extract(github_url, '(?:stargazers|stars)\\/([0-9,]+)', 1) AS BIGINT) DESC NULLS LAST,
         Name ASC,
         Version ASC
LIMIT 5;

WITH latest_versions AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    Links,
    row_number() OVER (
      PARTITION BY Name
      ORDER BY try_cast(regexp_extract(Version, '^(\\d+)\\.(\\d+)\\.(\\d+)', 1) AS INTEGER) DESC,
               try_cast(regexp_extract(Version, '^(\\d+)\\.(\\d+)\\.(\\d+)', 2) AS INTEGER) DESC,
               try_cast(regexp_extract(Version, '^(\\d+)\\.(\\d+)\\.(\\d+)', 3) AS INTEGER) DESC,
               Version DESC
    ) AS rn
  FROM packageinfo
), github_stars AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    CASE
      WHEN json_extract_string(Links, '$.github') IS NOT NULL THEN json_extract_string(Links, '$.github')
      WHEN json_extract_string(Links, '$.homepage') LIKE '%github.com%' THEN json_extract_string(Links, '$.homepage')
      ELSE NULL
    END AS github_url
  FROM latest_versions
  WHERE rn = 1
), stars_extracted AS (
  SELECT
    Name,
    Version,
    try_cast(regexp_extract(github_url, 'github\.com/[^/]+/[^/#?]+', 0) AS VARCHAR) AS repo_url,
    try_cast(regexp_extract(github_url, 'stars[=:/]?(\\d+)', 1) AS BIGINT) AS stars
  FROM github_stars
)
SELECT
  Name AS package_name,
  Version AS latest_version,
  stars AS github_star_number
FROM stars_extracted
WHERE stars IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY github_star_number DESC, package_name
LIMIT 5;

question: Among repositories that do not use Python, what proportion of their README.md files include copyright information?
system_prompt: Return one JSON object with key "sql": a single read-only SELECT (WITH allowed). DuckDB — use listed column names for this engine (may differ from PostgreSQL). Satisfy every part of the question: use enough columns/rows (GROUP BY/ORDER BY as needed); do not emit one scalar if multiple outputs are asked. SINGLE_TABLE: one scoped table or collection — prefer one FROM / one target collection.
user_prompt:
Q:
Among repositories that do not use Python, what proportion of their README.md files include copyright information?
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": [], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
The question asks for the proportion of repositories that do not use Python whose README.md files include copyright information. This can be answered within DuckDB by combining repository file listings with file contents: use files to identify repositories lacking Python and to locate README.md, then use contents to inspect README text for copyright mentions. No sqlite tables are needed for this t
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Repositories, contributors, stars — developer metadata; not consumer reviews.
SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "files",
      "columns": [
        "id",
        "mode",
        "path",
        "ref",
        "repo_name",
        "symlink_target"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Output one JSON object only: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}. Use only listed columns/keys.

llm_response.sql:
WITH repo_flags AS (
    SELECT
        repo_name,
        MAX(CASE WHEN LOWER(path) LIKE '%.py' OR LOWER(path) LIKE '%.pyw' OR LOWER(path) LIKE '%.ipy' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS has_python,
        MAX(CASE WHEN LOWER(path) = 'readme.md' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS has_readme,
        MAX(CASE WHEN LOWER(path) = 'readme.md' AND REGEXP_MATCHES(path, '.*', 'i') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS readme_present
    FROM files
    GROUP BY repo_name
), readme_repos AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT repo_name
    FROM files
    WHERE LOWER(path) = 'readme.md'
), no_python_repos AS (
    SELECT repo_name
    FROM repo_flags
    WHERE has_python = 0 AND has_readme = 1
), copyright_readmes AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT f.repo_name
    FROM files f
    INNER JOIN no_python_repos n
        ON f.repo_name = n.repo_name
    WHERE LOWER(f.path) = 'readme.md'
      AND LOWER(COALESCE(f.symlink_target, '')) NOT LIKE '%'
      AND 1 = 1
      AND EXISTS (
          SELECT 1
          FROM files f2
          WHERE f2.repo_name = f.repo_name
            AND LOWER(f2.path) = 'readme.md'
            AND (
                LOWER(f2.path) LIKE '%copyright%'
                OR LOWER(f2.repo_name) LIKE '%copyright%'
            )
      )
)
SELECT CAST(COUNT(DISTINCT c.repo_name) AS DOUBLE) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT n.repo_name), 0) AS sql
FROM no_python_repos n
LEFT JOIN copyright_readmes c
  ON c.repo_name = n.repo_name;

    repo_name,
    SUM(CASE WHEN lower(path) LIKE '%.py' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS python_files,

), readme_copyright AS (

  WHERE r.python_files = 0

)

            ELSE CAST(repos_with_copyright AS DOUBLE) / total_repos
       END AS proportion
FROM counts;

WITH repo_has_python AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT repo_name
  FROM files

),

  FROM files
  WHERE lower(path) = 'readme.md'
),
readme_copyright AS (
  SELECT f.repo_name,
         MAX(CASE
               WHEN lower(CAST(read_bytes(f.id) AS VARCHAR)) LIKE '%copyright%'
                 OR lower(CAST(read_text(f.id) AS VARCHAR)) LIKE '%copyright%'
               THEN 1 ELSE 0
             END) AS has_copyright
  FROM files f

  GROUP BY f.repo_name

  SELECT r.repo_name
  FROM repos_with_readme r
  LEFT JOIN repo_has_python p
    ON r.repo_name = p.repo_name
  WHERE p.repo_name IS NULL
)
SELECT
  CAST(SUM(COALESCE(rc.has_copyright, 0)) AS DOUBLE) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) AS proportion
FROM non_python_repos n
LEFT JOIN readme_copyright rc
  ON n.repo_name = rc.repo_name;

question: For LGG patients, compute the average log10-transformed expression of the IGF2 gene across different histology types. Only include patients with valid IGF2 expression values and histology annotations that are not enclosed in square brackets. Report the final average values with at least four decimal places of precision.
system_prompt: Return one JSON object with key "sql": a single read-only SELECT (WITH allowed). DuckDB — use listed column names for this engine (may differ from PostgreSQL). Satisfy every part of the question: use enough columns/rows (GROUP BY/ORDER BY as needed); do not emit one scalar if multiple outputs are asked. MULTI_TABLE: multiple scoped tables — use JOINs or subqueries as needed.
user_prompt:
Q:
For LGG patients, compute the average log10-transformed expression of the IGF2 gene across different histology types. Only include patients with valid IGF2 expression values and histology annotations that are not enclosed in square brackets. Report the final average values with at least four decimal places of precision.
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": ["average"], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
The question is a cancer genomics expression aggregation for LGG patients, which matches the PANCANCER_ATLAS duckdb molecular tables. Only RNASeq_Expression is needed to compute average log10-transformed IGF2 expression by histology type; no postgresql tables are relevant. A single engine is sufficient.
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Cancer genomics / TCGA-style facts — clinical and molecular tables.
LINKED_SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "Mutation_Data",
      "columns": [
        "CENTERS",
        "FILTER",
        "HGVSc",
        "HGVSp_Short",
        "Hugo_Symbol",
        "Normal_AliquotBarcode",
        "Normal_SampleBarcode",
        "Normal_SampleTypeLetterCode",
        "ParticipantBarcode",
        "Tumor_AliquotBarcode",
        "Tumor_SampleBarcode",
        "Variant_Classification"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "RNASeq_Expression",
      "columns": [
        "AliquotBarcode",
        "Entrez",
        "ParticipantBarcode",
        "SampleBarcode",
        "SampleType",
        "SampleTypeLetterCode",
        "Symbol",
        "normalized_count"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Generate one read-only query from CONTRACT + LINKED_SCHEMA only. OUTPUT_JSON: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}

llm_response.sql:
WITH lgg_clinical AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        ParticipantBarcode,
        CASE
            WHEN SampleType LIKE '%]' THEN NULL
            ELSE SampleType
        END AS histology_type
    FROM RNASeq_Expression
    WHERE SampleType IS NOT NULL
      AND SampleType NOT LIKE '[%'
      AND SampleType NOT LIKE '%]'
), igf2_expr AS (
    SELECT
        ParticipantBarcode,
        LOG10(normalized_count + 1) AS log10_igf2
    FROM RNASeq_Expression
    WHERE Symbol = 'IGF2'
      AND normalized_count IS NOT NULL
      AND normalized_count >= 0
)
SELECT
    c.histology_type,
    ROUND(AVG(e.log10_igf2), 4) AS avg_log10_igf2
FROM lgg_clinical AS c
JOIN igf2_expr AS e
  ON c.ParticipantBarcode = e.ParticipantBarcode
WHERE c.histology_type IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY c.histology_type
ORDER BY c.histology_type

Report the final average values with at least four decimal places of precision.
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": ["average"], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
The task is a cancer genomics expression analysis for LGG patients and requires only molecular expression data. DuckDB is the recommended primary analytical engine, and the relevant table in the authoritative registry is RNASeq_Expression. No joins to other engines are needed, and the PostgreSQL tables are unrelated to the question.
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Cancer genomics / TCGA-style facts — clinical and molecular tables.
LINKED_SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "Mutation_Data",
      "columns": [
        "CENTERS",
        "FILTER",
        "HGVSc",
        "HGVSp_Short",
        "Hugo_Symbol",
        "Normal_AliquotBarcode",
        "Normal_SampleBarcode",
        "Normal_SampleTypeLetterCode",
        "ParticipantBarcode",
        "Tumor_AliquotBarcode",
        "Tumor_SampleBarcode",
        "Variant_Classification"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "RNASeq_Expression",
      "columns": [
        "AliquotBarcode",
        "Entrez",
        "ParticipantBarcode",
        "SampleBarcode",

      ]
    }

  "collections": []

Generate one read-only query from CONTRACT + LINKED_SCHEMA only. OUTPUT_JSON: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}

llm_response.sql:
WITH igf2_lgg AS (
  SELECT
    ParticipantBarcode,
    CASE
      WHEN normalized_count IS NULL OR normalized_count <= 0 THEN NULL
      ELSE log10(normalized_count)
    END AS log10_expr,
    CASE
      WHEN SampleType IS NULL THEN NULL
      WHEN regexp_matches(SampleType, '^\\[.*\\]$') THEN NULL
      ELSE SampleType
    END AS histology_type
  FROM RNASeq_Expression
  WHERE Symbol = 'IGF2'
    AND ParticipantBarcode LIKE 'TCGA-%-LGG%'
)
SELECT
  histology_type,
  AVG(log10_expr) AS avg_log10_igf2_expression
FROM igf2_lgg
WHERE log10_expr IS NOT NULL
  AND histology_type IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY histology_type
ORDER BY histology_type;

ENGINE: duckdb
ROUTE:
The question is about stock index time series and asks for the Asia-region index with the highest average intraday volatility since 2020. The authoritative registry shows duckdb.index_trade contains the index-level trading time series needed to compute intraday volatility over time and compare indices. SQLite only exposes index_info, which is a snapshot table with Exchange/Currency and does not pr
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Stock index time series and constituents — financial analytics.
SCHEMA:
{
  "engine": "duckdb",
  "tables": [
    {
      "name": "index_trade",
      "columns": [
        "Adj Close",
        "Close",
        "CloseUSD",
        "Date",
        "High",
        "Index",
        "Low",
        "Open"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "collections": []
}
Output one JSON object only: SQL → {"sql":"..."} ; Mongo → {"collection":"...","pipeline":[...]}. Use only listed columns/keys.

llm_response.sql:
WITH asia_indices AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT "Index"
    FROM index_trade
    WHERE "Date" >= DATE '2020-01-01'
      AND (
        "Index" ILIKE '%Nikkei%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%Hang Seng%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%Shanghai%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%Shenzhen%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%KOSPI%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%SENSEX%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%NIFTY%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%ASX%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%Straits Times%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%Taiex%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%NZX%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%FTSE Bursa%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%BSE%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%Jakarta%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%Thailand%'
        OR "Index" ILIKE '%Philippines%'
      )
), vol AS (
    SELECT
        t."Index",
        AVG((t."High" - t."Low") / NULLIF(t."Open", 0)) AS avg_intraday_volatility
    FROM index_trade t
    JOIN asia_indices a
      ON t."Index" = a."Index"
    WHERE t."Date" >= DATE '2020-01-01'
    GROUP BY t."Index"
)
SELECT "Index"
FROM vol
ORDER BY avg_intraday_volatility DESC, "Index"
LIMIT 1;

WITH parsed AS (
  SELECT
    "Index" AS idx,
    CASE
      WHEN regexp_matches("Date", '^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}$') THEN CAST("Date" AS DATE)
      ELSE strptime("Date", '%d %b %Y, %H:%M')::DATE
    END AS dt,

      ELSE NULL

  FROM parsed

    AND intraday_vol IS NOT NULL
  GROUP BY idx
)

FROM avg_vol
ORDER BY avg_intraday_vol DESC
LIMIT 1;

WITH parsed AS (
  SELECT
    "Index",
    CASE
      WHEN try_strptime("Date", '%B %d, %Y at %I:%M %p') IS NOT NULL THEN try_strptime("Date", '%B %d, %Y at %I:%M %p')
      WHEN try_strptime("Date", '%d %b %Y, %H:%M') IS NOT NULL THEN try_strptime("Date", '%d %b %Y, %H:%M')

    "Low"

), vols AS (
  SELECT
    "Index",

  FROM parsed
  WHERE dt >= DATE '2020-01-01'

  GROUP BY "Index"
), ranked AS (

)

FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1;

WITH asia_indices AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT "Index"
  FROM index_trade
  WHERE "Index" LIKE '%Asia%'
     OR "Index" LIKE '%Japan%'
     OR "Index" LIKE '%Hong Kong%'
     OR "Index" LIKE '%China%'
     OR "Index" LIKE '%Korea%'
     OR "Index" LIKE '%Taiwan%'
     OR "Index" LIKE '%Singapore%'
     OR "Index" LIKE '%India%'
     OR "Index" LIKE '%Malaysia%'
     OR "Index" LIKE '%Thailand%'
     OR "Index" LIKE '%Indonesia%'
     OR "Index" LIKE '%Philippines%'
), volatility_by_index AS (
  SELECT
    it."Index",
    AVG( (it."High" - it."Low") / NULLIF(it."Open", 0) ) AS avg_intraday_volatility
  FROM index_trade it
  JOIN asia_indices ai
    ON it."Index" = ai."Index"
  WHERE CAST(it."Date" AS DATE) >= DATE '2020-01-01'
  GROUP BY it."Index"
)
SELECT "Index"
FROM volatility_by_index
ORDER BY avg_intraday_volatility DESC
LIMIT 1;

WITH parsed AS (
    SELECT
        "Index" AS idx,
        CASE
            WHEN regexp_matches("Date", '^[A-Za-z]+ [0-9]{2}, [0-9]{4} at [0-9]{1,2}:[0-9]{2} [AP]M$') THEN
                strptime("Date", '%B %d, %Y at %I:%M %p')
            WHEN regexp_matches("Date", '^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}$') THEN
                strptime("Date", '%Y-%m-%d')
            ELSE
                NULL
        END AS dt,
        Open,
        High,
        Low,
        Close
    FROM index_trade
),
filtered AS (
    SELECT
        idx,
        CASE
            WHEN Open IS NOT NULL AND Open <> 0 THEN (High - Low) / Open
            WHEN Close IS NOT NULL AND Close <> 0 THEN (High - Low) / Close
            ELSE NULL
        END AS intraday_volatility
    FROM parsed
    WHERE dt >= DATE '2020-01-01'
      AND dt IS NOT NULL
      AND idx IN ('Nikkei 225', 'Hang Seng', 'Shanghai Composite', 'S&P/ASX 200', 'KOSPI', 'Straits Times', 'NIFTY 50', 'BSE Sensex')
),
avg_vol AS (
    SELECT
        idx,
        AVG(intraday_volatility) AS avg_intraday_volatility
    FROM filtered
    WHERE intraday_volatility IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY idx
)
SELECT idx
FROM avg_vol
ORDER BY avg_intraday_volatility DESC
LIMIT 1;

user_prompt:
Q:
What is the average rating of all businesses located in Indianapolis, Indiana?
CONTRACT:
{"output_grain": "scalar", "metrics": ["average"], "filters": [], "dimensions": [], "time_bounds": [], "requires_join_or_group": false}
ENGINE: postgresql
ROUTE:
This is a simple location-based average rating question over businesses and their reviews. PostgreSQL contains the canonical relational Yelp business and review tables needed to filter businesses in Indianapolis, Indiana and aggregate their star ratings. No document data or cross-engine join is required, and business_category is unnecessary because the question is not about categories.
HINTS_JSON: {}
BENCHMARK_CONTEXT:
Yelp: reviews, businesses, users. PostgreSQL: table business_category has columns business_id and category (not category_name). business.attributes is a text dump of Yelp key/value data (often Python-dict-like), not English prose: parking questions must match keys like BikeParking and BusinessParking (e.g. substring '''BikeParking'': ''True''' or regex BusinessParking.*(garage|street|validated|lot|valet).*: True per scripts/reconcile_yelp_ground_truth.py q3). Do not use ILIKE '%bike parking%' / '%business parking%' — those phrases do not appear in attributes. review.date is often TEXT: filter years with review.date::date or CAST(review.date AS date). Reviews use stars; join business to review on business_id. Do not require JOIN business_category unless the question is about categories.
DATASET_HINTS:
- When joining a fact table to a dimension table on a shared id column, compare sample values in SCOPED_SCHEMA: if the foreign key uses a different string prefix than the referenced primary key in this benchmark slice, normalize in the join (e.g. REPLACE) so the join is not empty.
- Questions that ask which region has the most rows (reviews, orders, etc.) and also ask for an average for that region: compute per-region counts with GROUP BY, pick the region that wins the count, then average only within that region—not one AVG over the entire fact table.
- Semi-structured text fields (e.g. attributes) use vendor key/value layouts; align to keys and patterns implied by the question—do not rely on casual English phrases that may never appear in the stored string.

llm_response.sql:
SELECT AVG(r.stars) AS average_rating
FROM business b
JOIN review r ON r.business_id = b.business_id
WHERE b.description ILIKE '%Indianapolis%'
  AND b.state_code = 'IN';
